headline: All majors underwater on the 200-day; momentum diverging while whales move to cold storage and funding stays positive on leverage longs
paragraphs: The regime is broken. Bitcoin, Ethereum, Solana, XRP, BNB, Doge, and Cardano are all trading below their 200-day simple moving averages, and none of them have achieved a golden cross to signal a reversal underway. Bitcoin sits 48.2% below its 12-month high at 64,673. Ethereum is down 61% from its peak, Solana 68.7%, and Cardano, the laggard, trades 82.9% below its 12-month record. The 90-day ranges confirm the gravity: Bitcoin has spent the interval between 58,550 and 82,145, and Ethereum between 1,564 and 2,421. These are not tight consolidations; they are broad, wounded trading zones. The tape shows a market in downtrend by every major metric.

Momentum is sending a mixed signal against that trend. Bitcoin's RSI sits at 55, neutral neither overbought nor oversold, while its MACD stands above signal line, suggesting some stabilization in the decay. Ethereum's RSI is higher at 63.5, still above signal with MACD positive. By contrast, Solana's MACD has rolled below signal, the only major to do so, and its RSI of 52.6 is the lowest on the majors aside from XRP and Cardano. The interesting tension: three of the seven majors (BTC, ETH, XRP) still have MACD above signal despite being underwater on trend, which usually signals a bottom-building phase or the ghost of momentum that has not yet faded from the tape. Cardano stands out with the highest realized volatility at 72.9%, a sign of the whip-saw intensity in that name.

Leverage tells the story of who is still betting. Bitcoin funding sits at 0.0052% per 8 hours, equivalent to 5.7% annualized, which is not extreme but is firmly positive: longs are paying shorts for the privilege of holding the bet, a signal that aggregate leverage exposure remains tilted toward upside. Ethereum funding has flipped negative at -0.0033% per 8 hours, annualized at -3.7%, suggesting shorts are now drawing from the long side, a break in appetite. The positioning underneath reveals crowding: Bitcoin's long-to-short ratio on OKX is 1.37, Solana's jumps to 2.43, and Doge hits 3.28. These are lopsided books. In the past 24 hours, 1,588 BTC liquidations hit the tape, with shorts absorbing 88% of the blow at 12.9 million usd versus 1.7 million on longs; ETH liquidations ran 1,600 contracts with shorts again taking the heavier hit at 7.4 million versus 3.8 million on longs. The asymmetry suggests that aggressive short sellers have been caught off guard more than long holders, a detail that matters in thin markets.

Whale behavior is the most candid part of the tape. In the past 24 hours, 227.9 million usd of Bitcoin moved off exchanges on a net basis, driven by outflows of 409.6 million against inflows of only 181.7 million. The biggest off-exchange moves were 94.4 million from Coinbase Institutional, 82.6 million from OKEx, and 113.7 million of USDC leaving Kraken. This is not panic. Cold storage accumulation during extreme fear, while the chart shows major assets still posting small negative 24-hour returns and RSI readings that suggest no washout has yet occurred, is the tape's clearest contradiction to sentiment. The stablecoin float sits at 306.5 billion usd, down 1.91% over the month, which means dry powder has contracted slightly, but the whale off-exchange flow suggests that those who can afford to move coins are locking them away rather than liquidating them.

The Fear and Greed index reads 25, extreme fear, on a 30-day range that has bottomed at 11. Total market cap rose 2.58% in 24 hours despite the price action on the majors, an indication that smaller, more volatile names drove the bulk of the move; the gainers list shows PI, PUMP, and ZEC all up 12 to 15%, while the losers M, DEXE, and BDX fell 6 to 17%. Bitcoin dominance holds at 56.3%, stable. ETF flows remain positive: 181.1 million usd into Bitcoin ETFs on July 14, and 58.3 million into Ethereum ETFs, with cumulative flows of 51.1 billion and 11.0 billion respectively since inception. The traditional finance bid has not wavered despite the regime breakdown.

Watch for three conditions to flag a change: first, a reclaim of the 200-day line by at least two or three of the majors, which would break the unified downtrend; second, a funding collapse on Bitcoin below zero, which would signal that the long leverage crowd is finally capitulating; and third, a reversal in the whale off-exchange pattern, either to inflows or a stalling of outflows, which would suggest the edge has moved back to the sell side. Until one of those conditions appears in the tape, the market remains in downtrend with crowded leverage on the long side and experienced money moving quietly to storage.
